Covers his early career in the Netherlands (The Potato Eaters), the transformative influence of Impressionism and Japanese prints in Paris, his productive period in Arles, the relationship with Gauguin, his mental illness and hospitalisation at Saint-Remy, and his death at Auvers-sur-Oise. Examines his innovative use of colour and brushwork, his enormous posthumous influence on Expressionism and modern art, and the mythology of the tormented genius. Connects to art history, Post-Impressionism, psychology, and the culture of artistic biography.

episodes.title: Virgil's Georgics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the Roman poet's celebration of agriculture and rural life
episodes.summaryAI: Virgil's Georgics (c.29 BC), the Roman poet's four-book didactic poem on agriculture and rural life. Covers the poem's ostensible subjects (crops, trees and vines, livestock, beekeeping) and its deeper themes of humanity's relationship with nature, labour as moral virtue, the aftermath of civil war, and the golden age myth. Examines the Georgics' literary artistry, its Hesiodic and Lucretian influences, the famous Orpheus and Eurydice episode, and the poem's political dimension (Augustan ideology of Italian renewal). Connects to Latin poetry, agricultural history, Roman politics, pastoral and didactic literary traditions, and environmental humanities.

episodes.title: Virtue
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg explores the meaning and purpose of the philosophical concept of virtue.
episodes.summaryAI: The concept of virtue in philosophy, tracing its development from ancient Greek arete through Christian moral theology to modern virtue ethics. Covers Socrates's identification of virtue with knowledge, Plato's cardinal virtues (wisdom, courage, temperance, justice), Aristotle's character virtues and the doctrine of the mean, Stoic and Epicurean virtue, Aquinas's theological virtues (faith, hope, charity), Hume's sentimentalism, and the modern revival of virtue ethics (Anscombe, MacIntyre). Examines the relationship between virtue, happiness (eudaimonia), and human flourishing. Connects to moral philosophy, ancient philosophy, theology, and ethical theory.

episodes.title: Vitalism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the 18th and 19th century quest for the spark of life.
episodes.summaryAI: Vitalism, the 18th and 19th-century doctrine that living organisms possess a vital force or principle distinct from physical and chemical processes. Covers the Montpellier School (Barthez), Stahl's animism, Bichat's distinction between vital and physical properties, the challenge from organic chemistry (Wohler's urea synthesis 1828), the cell theory, and the eventual triumph of mechanistic biology. Examines vitalism's relationship to Romantic Naturphilosophie, the origins of biology as a discipline, and whether residual vitalist intuitions persist in modern debates about consciousness and emergence. Connects to the history of biology, philosophy of science, chemistry, and the mind-body problem.

episodes.title: Vitruvius and De Architectura
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Vitruvius's De Architectura.
episodes.summaryAI: Vitruvius's De Architectura (c.30-15 BC), the only surviving architectural treatise from classical antiquity. Covers the ten books addressing town planning, building materials, temple design, civil and domestic architecture, water supply, clocks, and military machines, Vitruvius's famous principles (firmitas, utilitas, venustas - solidity, utility, beauty), and the Vitruvian Man concept of ideal human proportions. Examines the treatise's rediscovery in the Renaissance and its enormous influence on Alberti, Palladio, and Neoclassical architecture. Connects to Roman architecture, architectural theory, Renaissance humanism, and the classical tradition in Western design.

episodes.title: Voltaire's Candide
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss Voltaire's satirical novel Candide, published in 1759.
episodes.summaryAI: Voltaire's Candide (1759), the satirical novella attacking Leibnizian optimism ('the best of all possible worlds'). Covers the plot's picaresque journey through disasters (Lisbon earthquake, war, slavery, religious persecution), the characters of Pangloss, Cunegonde, and Martin, the conclusion ('we must cultivate our garden'), and Voltaire's targets including philosophical optimism, religious intolerance, and colonial violence. Examines Candide's relationship to the Enlightenment critique of theodicy, its literary form, and its enduring influence on satirical fiction. Connects to Enlightenment philosophy, French literature, satire, the problem of evil, and the history of ideas.

episodes.title: Voyages of James Cook
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the voyages of James Cook, as suggested by listeners.
episodes.summaryAI: The voyages of Captain James Cook (1728-1779), the British explorer who led three Pacific expeditions that transformed European knowledge of the Pacific Ocean. Covers the first voyage (1768-71) to observe the Transit of Venus and map New Zealand and eastern Australia, the second voyage (1772-75) circumnavigating Antarctica and disproving the southern continent, and the third voyage (1776-79) seeking the Northwest Passage, ending with Cook's death in Hawaii. Examines the scientific contributions (botany, cartography, scurvy prevention), encounters with indigenous peoples, and the expeditions' role in British imperialism. Connects to exploration, navigation, natural history, and colonial encounters.

episodes.title: Vulcanology
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the formation and eruption of volcanoes.
episodes.summaryAI: Vulcanology, the scientific study of volcanoes, their formation, eruption mechanisms, and geological significance. Covers the types of volcanoes (shield, stratovolcano, caldera), the relationship between volcanism and plate tectonics (subduction zones, mid-ocean ridges, hotspots), the chemistry of magma, eruption styles (effusive versus explosive), famous eruptions (Vesuvius, Krakatoa, Mount St Helens), volcanic hazards (pyroclastic flows, lahars, tephra), and the role of volcanism in shaping Earth's atmosphere and climate. Examines volcanic monitoring and prediction efforts. Connects to geology, plate tectonics, earth science, hazard management, and climate history.

episodes.title: Wagner
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the position of Richard Wagner and his music in German culture.
episodes.summaryAI: Richard Wagner (1813-1883), the German composer whose revolutionary music dramas (the Ring cycle, Tristan und Isolde, Parsifal) transformed opera and influenced the course of Western music. Covers Wagner's concept of Gesamtkunstwerk (total artwork), leitmotif technique, harmonic innovations (the Tristan chord), the Bayreuth Festival Theatre, and his cultural significance in German nationalism. Examines Wagner's controversial legacy including his antisemitic writings, the appropriation of his music by the Nazis, and ongoing debates about separating art from artist. Connects to music history, German cultural identity, nationalism, aesthetics, and the politics of art.

episodes.title: Walt Whitman
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the innovative and highly influential American poet.
episodes.summaryAI: Walt Whitman (1819-1892), the American poet whose Leaves of Grass (1855, revised throughout his life) revolutionised poetry with its free verse form and democratic vision. Covers Whitman's innovations in prosody (abandoning metre and rhyme for catalogues, long lines, and incantatory rhythm), his celebration of the body and sexuality, the relationship between individual and democratic community, his Civil War poetry (Drum-Taps, 'O Captain! My Captain!'), and his influence on modernist and Beat poetry. Examines Whitman as the quintessential poet of American democracy and identity. Connects to American literature, poetics, democracy, sexuality, and the history of the self.

episodes.title: Walter Benjamin
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the most influential thinkers of the last century.
episodes.summaryAI: Walter Benjamin (1892-1940), the German-Jewish cultural critic and philosopher associated with the Frankfurt School. Covers his major works including The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction (aura, authenticity in mass culture), The Arcades Project (Paris as capital of the 19th century), the Theses on the Philosophy of History (the angel of history, messianic time), and his literary criticism (Baudelaire, Kafka, Proust). Examines Benjamin's unique blend of Marxism, Jewish mysticism, and literary theory, his concept of the flaneur, and his tragic death fleeing the Nazis. Connects to critical theory, aesthetics, cultural criticism, and modernism.

episodes.title: War in the 20th Century
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg explores ideas that have influenced 20th century human rights and warfare.
episodes.summaryAI: War in the 20th century, examining the ideas that have shaped modern warfare, human rights, and international humanitarian law. Covers the transformation from limited to total war, the impact of industrial technology on warfare, the Geneva Conventions, the Nuremberg principles, nuclear weapons and deterrence theory, guerrilla warfare and decolonisation, the concept of just war theory in modern contexts, and the emergence of international humanitarian law. Examines whether the laws of war can constrain conflict, the tension between military necessity and civilian protection, and the legacy of 20th-century violence for human rights discourse. Connects to military history, international law, ethics, and political philosophy.

episodes.title: Washington and the American Revolution
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the first President of the US and what drove him to revolution.
episodes.summaryAI: George Washington (1732-1799) and the American Revolution, examining the first US President's transformation from Virginia planter and British colonial officer to revolutionary leader. Covers Washington's command of the Continental Army, key military decisions (crossing the Delaware, Valley Forge, Yorktown), his political vision for republican government, the Constitutional Convention, his precedent-setting presidency (two-term limit, civilian control of military), and the construction of his legacy. Examines the tension between Washington as slaveholder and champion of liberty, and his role in shaping American political culture. Connects to the American Revolution, constitutional history, and political leadership.

episodes.title: Water
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss water, one of the most remarkable of all molecules.
episodes.summaryAI: Water (H2O), one of the most remarkable and anomalous molecules in nature. Covers water's unique properties: hydrogen bonding, high specific heat capacity, expansion upon freezing, universal solvent capacity, surface tension, and capillary action. Examines water's role in biochemistry (medium for life), its phase transitions, the water cycle and climate regulation, the structure of ice, anomalous density behaviour, and open questions about water's liquid-state structure (polyamorphism debate). Connects to chemistry, physics, biology, climate science, and planetary science.

episodes.title: Weber's The Protestant Ethic
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Weber's The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism.
episodes.summaryAI: Max Weber's The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism (1905), the influential sociological thesis linking Calvinist religious values to the rise of modern capitalism. Covers Weber's argument that Protestant (especially Calvinist) ideas about predestination, worldly asceticism, and the calling (Beruf) created a psychological incentive for disciplined economic activity and capital accumulation. Examines critiques from Marxists (base versus superstructure), historians (capitalism predates Protestantism), and comparative scholars (capitalism in non-Protestant societies), and Weber's broader project on rationalisation. Connects to sociology, economic history, religious studies, and the origins of modernity.

episodes.title: Welcoming Misha Glenny
episodes.synopsis: Misha Glenny introduces himself as he prepares for his first episode of In Our Time.
episodes.summaryAI: An introductory episode in which Misha Glenny presents himself as the new host of In Our Time, preparing to succeed Melvyn Bragg. A brief transitional programme rather than a substantive scholarly discussion. Covers the format and mission of In Our Time as a programme dedicated to the history of ideas, and signals the programme's continuation under new leadership. Connects to media history, intellectual broadcasting, and the BBC's public service mission.

episodes.title: Wilberforce
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the life of anti-slavery campaigner, William Wilberforce.
episodes.summaryAI: William Wilberforce (1759-1833), the British politician and evangelical Christian who led the parliamentary campaign against the Atlantic slave trade. Covers Wilberforce's conversion to evangelical Christianity, his alliance with the Clapham Sect, the decades-long campaign resulting in the Slave Trade Act (1807) and the Slavery Abolition Act (1833), his methods of parliamentary persuasion and public mobilisation, and debates about his relative importance versus grassroots abolitionist movements. Examines the relationship between religious conviction and social reform, and critiques of the 'great man' narrative of abolition. Connects to British political history, abolitionism, evangelicalism, and human rights.

episodes.title: Wilfred Owen
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss one of the outstanding poets of the First World War.
episodes.summaryAI: Wilfred Owen (1893-1918), one of the most important poets of the First World War, killed just one week before the Armistice. Covers his major poems (Dulce et Decorum Est, Anthem for Doomed Youth, Strange Meeting, Futility), his development of pararhyme and other technical innovations, the influence of Keats and Sassoon, his experiences of trench warfare and shell shock, and his declaration that 'the Poetry is in the pity.' Examines Owen's posthumous reputation, the editing by Sassoon and Blunden, and his influence on anti-war poetry and British cultural memory of WWI. Connects to war poetry, English literature, and World War I history.

episodes.title: William and Caroline Herschel
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the siblings at the forefront of 18th-century astronomy
episodes.summaryAI: William Herschel (1738-1822) and his sister Caroline Herschel (1750-1848), the Hanoverian-born astronomers who revolutionised observational astronomy in Britain. Covers William's discovery of Uranus (1781), the first new planet found since antiquity, his construction of the largest telescopes of the age, his mapping of nebulae and the Milky Way, his discovery of infrared radiation, and Caroline's independent discoveries of comets and nebulae, making her the first professional female astronomer. Examines their collaborative working methods, the patronage of George III, and their legacy for stellar astronomy. Connects to the history of astronomy, women in science, and the scientific revolution.

episodes.title: William Cecil
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the most powerful man in the court of Elizabeth I.
episodes.summaryAI: William Cecil, Lord Burghley (1520-1598), the chief minister to Queen Elizabeth I for forty years and the most powerful figure in Elizabethan government. Covers Cecil's role in shaping the Elizabethan religious settlement, managing Anglo-Spanish relations, intelligence networks against Catholic plots (Walsingham), the execution of Mary Queen of Scots, and his promotion of Protestant England as a European power. Examines Cecil's political methods, his dynasty-building (his son Robert Cecil succeeded him), and his significance for the development of the English state. Connects to Tudor political history, the Reformation, espionage, and statecraft.

episodes.title: William Hazlitt
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss the life and works of the essayist William Hazlitt.
episodes.summaryAI: William Hazlitt (1778-1830), the English essayist and literary critic of the Romantic period. Covers Hazlitt's major works (The Spirit of the Age, Characters of Shakespeare's Plays, Table-Talk), his passionate prose style, his radical political sympathies (admiration for Napoleon, defence of the French Revolution), his literary criticism championing Shakespeare and the Elizabethans, and his personal relationships with Wordsworth, Coleridge, and Lamb. Examines Hazlitt's significance as a pioneer of modern literary journalism and the personal essay, and his relative neglect compared to contemporaries. Connects to Romantic literature, literary criticism, political radicalism, and the history of the essay.

episodes.title: William James's 'The Varieties of Religious Experience'
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss The Varieties of Religious Experience by William James.
episodes.summaryAI: William James's The Varieties of Religious Experience (1902), the landmark study of religion based on individual psychological experience rather than institutional theology. Covers James's pragmatist approach, his analysis of conversion experiences, mysticism, saintliness, and the divided self, his distinction between healthy-minded and sick-souled religion, and his argument that religious experience should be judged by its fruits rather than its origins. Examines the book's influence on psychology of religion, phenomenology, and liberal theology, and James's broader philosophical project. Connects to psychology, philosophy of religion, pragmatism, and the study of mysticism.

episodes.title: William Morris
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss a great cultural figure of the 19th century.
episodes.summaryAI: William Morris (1834-1896), the Victorian artist, designer, writer, and socialist activist. Covers Morris's contributions to the Arts and Crafts movement (wallpaper, textile, and furniture design opposing industrial mass production), his poetry and prose romances, the Kelmscott Press's revival of fine book printing, his Socialist League activism and utopian vision (News from Nowhere), and his influence on design, architecture, and environmental thinking. Examines the tension between Morris's medievalist aesthetics and his radical politics, and his legacy for modern design and environmentalism. Connects to art and design, socialism, Victorian culture, and the relationship between aesthetics and politics.

episodes.title: Witchcraft
episodes.synopsis: The history of witchcraft in Reformation Europe.
episodes.summaryAI: Witchcraft in Reformation Europe (c.1450-1750), the period of intense witch persecution that led to the execution of an estimated 40,000-60,000 people, predominantly women. Covers the intellectual framework of the Malleus Maleficarum, the demonological theories linking witchcraft to Satan, the social profile of the accused (older women, marginalised), the role of confessional conflict in intensifying prosecution, regional variations, the witch trial procedures (torture, spectral evidence), and the eventual decline of persecution with Enlightenment rationalism. Examines gender, power, and scapegoating dynamics. Connects to early modern history, gender studies, religion, and the sociology of deviance.

episodes.title: Wittgenstein
episodes.synopsis: The life, work and legacy of the 20th century philosopher Ludwig Wittgenstein.
episodes.summaryAI: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889-1951), one of the most influential philosophers of the 20th century. Covers the early philosophy of the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (picture theory of meaning, limits of language, the mystical), his abandonment of philosophy and return to Cambridge, the later philosophy of the Philosophical Investigations (language games, family resemblance, private language argument, rule-following), and his impact on analytic philosophy, philosophy of language, and philosophy of mind. Examines the dramatic shift between early and late Wittgenstein, and his broader legacy for understanding meaning and understanding. Connects to philosophy of language, logic, epistemology, and the history of analytic philosophy.

episodes.title: Women and Enlightenment Science
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the role played by women in Enlightenment science.
episodes.summaryAI: Women and Enlightenment science, examining the significant but often overlooked contributions of women to 18th-century scientific practice. Covers the roles of women as translators (Emilie du Chatelet's translation of Newton), illustrators (Maria Sibylla Merian's entomological art), astronomers (Caroline Herschel), salon hostesses facilitating scientific exchange, and popular science writers. Examines the barriers women faced (exclusion from universities and learned societies), the domestic spaces where science was practiced, and how gender norms both constrained and enabled women's participation. Connects to the history of science, women's history, the Enlightenment, and the sociology of knowledge.

episodes.title: Work in the 20th Century
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the changing ideas about the function of work in the 20th century.
episodes.summaryAI: Work in the 20th century, examining the changing ideas about the purpose, organisation, and meaning of work. Covers Taylorism and scientific management, Fordist mass production, the welfare state and labour rights, the service economy transition, automation anxieties, the rise of knowledge work, feminist critiques of unpaid domestic labour, and post-industrial debates about work-life balance, the end of full employment, and universal basic income proposals. Examines how work relates to identity, social status, and human flourishing, and whether the work ethic remains viable. Connects to economics, sociology, labour history, and political philosophy.

episodes.title: Wormholes
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests test the idea that there are shortcuts between distant galaxies.
episodes.summaryAI: Wormholes, the hypothetical tunnels through spacetime connecting distant points in the universe. Covers the Einstein-Rosen bridge (1935), Morris and Thorne's traversable wormholes requiring exotic matter with negative energy density, the relationship to general relativity and the topology of spacetime, the theoretical possibility of time travel via wormholes, and the quantum physics constraints (Casimir effect, quantum energy conditions). Examines whether wormholes can actually exist in nature, their appearance in science fiction, and what they reveal about our understanding of spacetime structure. Connects to general relativity, quantum gravity, cosmology, and the philosophy of time.

episodes.title: Writing and Political Oppression
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines whether writers have a political role in modern society.
episodes.summaryAI: Writing and political oppression, examining whether writers have a political responsibility and what role literature plays in resistance to authoritarian regimes. Covers the tradition of dissident literature (Solzhenitsyn, Havel, Rushdie), censorship and self-censorship, the relationship between aesthetics and political commitment, the writer as witness, PEN International and freedom of expression campaigns, and the power of literature to preserve memory and articulate alternative realities under repression. Examines tensions between artistic autonomy and political engagement, and whether literature can effect political change. Connects to literary theory, political philosophy, human rights, and the sociology of literature.

episodes.title: Wuthering Heights
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Emily Bronte's only novel, Wuthering Heights.
episodes.summaryAI: Emily Bronte's Wuthering Heights (1847), the only novel by one of the most enigmatic figures in English literature. Covers the passionate and destructive relationship between Heathcliff and Catherine Earnshaw, the novel's innovative narrative structure (multiple narrators, complex chronology), the Yorkshire moorland setting, themes of nature versus civilisation, social class, obsessive love, and revenge across generations, and the novel's initially hostile reception. Examines the novel's Gothic elements, its resistance to conventional moral frameworks, and its reassessment as a masterpiece of world literature. Connects to Victorian literature, the Gothic, feminist criticism, and the Bronte literary family.

episodes.title: Wyclif and the Lollards
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss John Wyclif and the Lollards.
episodes.summaryAI: John Wyclif (c.1330-1384) and the Lollard movement, examining the Oxford theologian's challenge to medieval Catholic orthodoxy. Covers Wyclif's doctrines: the primacy of Scripture, rejection of transubstantiation, critique of papal authority and clerical wealth, advocacy for vernacular Bible translation, and his concept of dominion by grace. Examines the Lollard movement that continued after his death, their persecution under De Heretico Comburendo (1401), and Wyclif's influence on Jan Hus and the later Protestant Reformation. Connects to medieval theology, church reform, the origins of Protestantism, English political history, and the history of biblical translation.

episodes.title: Xenophon
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and his guests discuss the ancient Greek historian and soldier Xenophon.
episodes.summaryAI: Xenophon (c.430-354 BC), the ancient Greek historian, soldier, and pupil of Socrates. Covers his major works: the Anabasis (the march of the Ten Thousand Greek mercenaries through Persian territory), the Hellenica (continuing Thucydides's history), the Memorabilia and Symposium (accounts of Socrates differing from Plato's), and the Cyropaedia (idealised education of Cyrus the Great). Examines Xenophon's military experience, his political conservatism and admiration for Sparta, his significance as a historical source, and his rehabilitation from long critical neglect. Connects to ancient Greek history, historiography, military history, and Socratic philosophy.

episodes.title: Yeats and Irish Politics
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the effect of Irish politics on the work of the poet W.B. Yeats.
episodes.summaryAI: W.B. Yeats (1865-1939) and Irish politics, examining how Ireland's turbulent history shaped the poetry of its greatest modern poet. Covers Yeats's involvement in the Irish Literary Revival and Celtic Twilight, his relationship with Maud Gonne and Irish nationalism, his responses to the Easter Rising ('Easter 1916'), the War of Independence, and the Civil War, his service as a Free State senator, and the tension between his aristocratic elitism and democratic nationalism. Examines poems including 'September 1913,' 'The Second Coming,' and 'Meditations in Time of Civil War.' Connects to Irish literature, nationalism, political poetry, and modernism.

episodes.title: Yeats and Mysticism
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg explores the strange and mystical world of the Irish poet W B Yeats.
episodes.summaryAI: W.B. Yeats and mysticism, exploring the Irish poet's deep engagement with occult and esoteric traditions. Covers Yeats's membership in the Golden Dawn, his interest in theosophy, spiritualism, and automatic writing (A Vision), the influence of Blake, Swedenborg, and Indian philosophy, the symbolic system of gyres and phases of the moon, and how mystical beliefs shaped his poetry and drama. Examines the relationship between Yeats's occultism and his modernist poetic achievement, and the scholarly debate about whether his mysticism was sincere belief or poetic strategy. Connects to modernist literature, occultism, comparative mysticism, and Irish cultural history.

episodes.title: Youth
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the history of concepts and ideas on youth from antiquity to today.
episodes.summaryAI: The history of concepts and ideas about youth from antiquity to the present. Covers ancient Greek and Roman attitudes (ephebes, the gymnasium), medieval concepts of the ages of man, the Romantic invention of adolescence as a distinct phase, Rousseau's Emile and the idealisation of childhood innocence, Victorian anxieties about juvenile delinquency, the emergence of youth culture in the 20th century (teenagers, counterculture), and contemporary debates about extended adolescence. Examines how definitions of youth reflect broader social values about education, sexuality, responsibility, and generational identity. Connects to social history, psychology, education, and cultural studies.

episodes.title: Zen
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg and guests discuss Zen, a distinctively East Asian form of Buddhism.
episodes.summaryAI: Zen Buddhism, the distinctively East Asian form of Buddhism emphasising direct experience and meditation (zazen) over textual study and doctrinal elaboration. Covers Zen's origins in Chinese Chan Buddhism (Bodhidharma's legendary arrival from India), its synthesis of Buddhist and Daoist elements, the development of koans and sudden enlightenment (satori/kensho), the Rinzai and Soto schools, Zen's profound influence on Japanese culture (tea ceremony, calligraphy, garden design, martial arts), and its 20th-century transmission to the West (Suzuki, Beat Generation). Connects to Buddhism, Japanese culture, meditation practice, aesthetics, and comparative religion.

episodes.title: Zeno's Paradoxes
episodes.synopsis: Does an arrow in flight move and could Achilles overtake a tortoise? Not according to Zeno
episodes.summaryAI: Zeno's Paradoxes, the set of philosophical puzzles attributed to Zeno of Elea (c.490-430 BC) challenging the coherence of motion and plurality. Covers the major paradoxes: Achilles and the Tortoise, the Dichotomy, the Arrow, and the Stadium. Examines the paradoxes' engagement with infinity, continuity, and the nature of space and time, Aristotle's responses, the modern mathematical resolution through calculus and the theory of infinite series, and the continuing philosophical debate about whether mathematical solutions truly dissolve the conceptual puzzles. Connects to ancient philosophy, mathematics, philosophy of space and time, and the foundations of calculus.

episodes.title: Zero
episodes.synopsis: Melvyn Bragg examines the number between 1 and -1, once denounced as the devil's work.
episodes.summaryAI: Zero, the number between one and negative one, tracing its conceptual history from Babylonian placeholder through Indian mathematics (Brahmagupta's rules for zero arithmetic), its transmission via Arabic mathematics to medieval Europe, and the resistance it encountered from those who saw it as a dangerous or diabolical concept. Covers zero's revolutionary impact on the place-value number system, its role in algebra and calculus, its philosophical implications (representing nothing, the void), and its connection to infinity. Examines the difficulty of defining zero and its operations (division by zero). Connects to mathematics, the history of numeracy, philosophy, and cross-cultural intellectual exchange.

episodes.title: Zoroastrianism
episodes.synopsis: The history of Zoroastrianism, claimed to be the first monotheistic religion.
episodes.summaryAI: Zoroastrianism, the ancient Persian religion founded by the prophet Zarathustra (Zoroaster), often claimed as the world's first monotheistic faith. Covers the central beliefs: Ahura Mazda as supreme deity, the cosmic struggle between good (Asha) and evil (Druj), the importance of good thoughts, words, and deeds, fire as a sacred symbol, eschatological judgment, and the influence on Judaism, Christianity, and Islam (heaven, hell, angels, resurrection, messianism). Examines Zoroastrianism's role as the state religion of the Achaemenid and Sassanid empires, the surviving Parsi community, and its contribution to the history of monotheism. Connects to comparative religion, Persian history, and theology.
